summary: This article provides a comprehensive guide to semiconductor Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) available to UK investors, explaining what an ETF is, the history and importance of semiconductors, and how to evaluate investment opportunities in this sector. It highlights top-performing semiconductor ETFs, discusses the significance of UCITS, and addresses considerations for ESG-minded investors, particularly concerning ON Semiconductor.

title: Adas/Connected Car - June 2022 v1
summary: The "ADAS/Connected Car - June 2022" special report details advancements in automotive technology, focusing on advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS) and the critical need for high-speed data transmission within vehicles. It addresses the challenges of integrating high-resolution cameras and radars, demanding greater bandwidth and lower latency in in-vehicle networks, and highlights the role of edge inference computers and radar technology in enhancing vehicle safety and performance. The report emphasizes the importance of testing solutions, processing capabilities, and continuous innovation in shaping the future of connected vehicles.

title: ASML engineer who fled charges of stealing chip tech is living comfortably in China as CEO of XTAL Inc.
summary: A former ASML engineer, Zongchang Yu, accused of stealing intellectual property, is now serving as CEO of XTAL Inc. in China. Yu founded two companies, XTAL Inc. and DongFang JingYuan Electron, both facing IP theft allegations from ASML. Despite lawsuits and a massive uncollectable fine against XTAL, Yu fled to China and reportedly runs DongFang with government support, benefiting from patents that include the contested OPC software.

title: Gen-trepreneur Z is making its mark on the future of small business
summary: A Microsoft Store report "Small Business State of Mind" highlights how Generation Z is redefining entrepreneurship. Gen Z small business owners prioritize social good, work unconventional hours, and are using platforms like TikTok for business advice, aiming for early retirement rather than traditional corporate paths. The article emphasizes their unique perspective on work, success, and the future of small business, despite challenges like inflation and data security risks.

title: AMD unveils roadmap for next-gen Ryzen chips and Zen 5 architecture
summary: AMD has revealed its roadmap for upcoming desktop, laptop, and mobile processors during its Financial Analyst Day event. The company detailed performance improvements for the Ryzen 7000 desktop chips and discussed the Zen 5 architecture slated for 2024. AMD also highlighted its Phoenix Point mobile CPUs featuring RDNA-3 graphics and an AI engine from Xilinx.

title: AMD Details Strategy to Drive Next Phase of Growth Across $300 Billion Market for High-Performance and Adaptive Computing Solutions
summary: AMD unveiled its strategy for future growth at its Financial Analyst Day, focusing on an expanded portfolio of high-performance and adaptive computing products across data center, embedded, client, and gaming markets. The company detailed next-generation CPU and GPU roadmaps, including "Zen 4" and "Zen 5" CPUs, RDNA 3 graphics, and CDNA 3 architecture for AI. AMD also announced new financial reporting segments to better align with its strategic end markets and introduced a new brand platform, "together we advance_".

title: AMD Reveals Roadmap for Zen 4-based laptop CPU, Zen 5, RDNA 3, and RDNA 4
summary: AMD has unveiled its roadmap for upcoming processors and graphics architectures, including the Zen 4-based Ryzen 7000 series, Zen 5, and RDNA 3, with RDNA 4 slated for 2024. The Zen 4 architecture promises significant performance and efficiency gains, competing with Intel's offerings, while Zen 5 will be a ground-up redesign with AI and machine learning enhancements. The RDNA 3 graphics architecture will be chipset-based and on a 5nm architecture, aiming for 50 percent better performance per watt than its predecessor.

title: Advanced Micro Devices Inc. stock underperforms Monday when compared to competitors
summary: Shares of Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD) fell 8.26% on Monday, closing at $86.99, amidst a general market downturn with the S&P 500 and Dow Jones also dropping. The stock recorded its seventh consecutive day of losses and underperformed against competitors such as NVIDIA Corp., Intel Corp., and Texas Instruments Inc. Trading volume for AMD was also below its 50-day average.

title: Amid domestic semiconductor wave, Texas Instruments begins $30B build
summary: Texas Instruments has broken ground on an estimated $30 billion semiconductor manufacturing facility in Sherman, Texas, which is the largest private sector investment in Texas history. Production is slated to begin in 2025, creating up to 3,000 direct jobs and contributing to a nationwide effort to increase domestic chip manufacturing. This project aligns with a broader industry trend of expanding semiconductor fabrication in the U.S., driven by rising demand and supply chain challenges.

title: Who's growing faster than Nvidia and AMD? Rising datacenter star Marvell
summary: Marvell Technology significantly outpaced other major fabless chip designers like AMD, Nvidia, and Qualcomm in the first quarter, achieving 72% semiconductor revenue growth. This rapid growth was largely driven by its acquisition of Innovium, a switch ASIC business catering to hyperscale data centers. Marvell is strategically shifting its focus from consumer markets to data infrastructure, with datacenter products now accounting for 44% of its total revenue, reinforcing its long-term growth strategy in cloud, 5G, and automotive sectors.

title: With Funding Stalled, Chip Makers Warn Congress the U.S. Is Lagging
summary: Chip makers are urging Congress to pass the CHIPS Act, which would provide over $52 billion to build semiconductor factories in the U.S. They warn that further delays could lead them to move manufacturing overseas, putting the U.S. at a disadvantage against other nations and exacerbating the global chip shortage. The Biden administration views this legislation as critical for American jobs and addressing supply chain issues.

title: Opinion | Semiconductor Dependency Imperils American Security
summary: The article argues that the U.S.'s heavy reliance on Taiwan for advanced semiconductor manufacturing poses a significant national security risk, particularly in light of potential Chinese dominance. While some question the effectiveness of U.S. subsidies to foster domestic chip leadership, the authors emphasize that reducing dependence on TSMC is crucial to protect America's technology sector and military capabilities. A disruption in Taiwan's chip production could severely impact the U.S.

title: These software companies are unsung winners in the semiconductor industry
summary: This article highlights software companies like Synopsys Inc. (SNPS) and Cadence Design Systems Inc. (CDNS) as critical but often overlooked players in the semiconductor industry. These companies provide Electronic Design Automation (EDA) software, which is essential for chip design engineers. Despite the focus on chip giants, these software firms play a crucial role in enabling the broader semiconductor sector, especially amidst ongoing chip shortages.

title: Adobe Acrobat Blocking 30 Security Apps From Scanning PDFs
summary: Adobe Acrobat Reader has been found to actively block 30 different security applications from scanning PDF files by preventing DLL injection into its processes. Adobe claims this is due to incompatibility with their Chromium-based engine (CEF), but other programs using CEF do not exhibit this behavior. This issue raises concerns about security vulnerabilities in PDFs, prompting some to suggest finding alternative PDF readers while Adobe works on a solution.

title: Chinese DPU chip firm gains funds, unicorn status ...
summary: Jaguar Microsystems, a Chinese Data Processing Unit (DPU) chip company founded in 2020 by former Broadcom executive Sunny Siu, has secured hundreds of millions of yuan in funding, propelling its valuation to unicorn status at approximately US$1.34 billion. The financing round was led by Tencent Holdings Ltd. and included other major investors. Jaguar Microsystems, which employs nearly 400 staff, released an FPGA version of its DPU in 2021 and plans for mass production of its system-chip version by 2023, primarily targeting cloud computing and internet companies, with Tencent expected to be a key customer.

title: DDR5: How Faster Memory Speeds Shape The Future
summary: This article discusses the advancements and challenges brought by DDR5 memory, which doubles the data rate of its predecessor, DDR4. It highlights how DDR5's increased speeds and power efficiency enable emerging technologies like IoT and V2X autonomous vehicles. The article also touches upon the future of DDR6, anticipating further speed increases and new signaling innovations to overcome physical limitations.

title: AMD Powers Real-Time AI Processing at the Edge for Revolutionary Canon Free Viewpoint Video System, Transforming Live Sports Broadcasts
summary: AMD announced that its Versal AI Core series, with AMD AI Engine technology, has been selected by Canon for its Free Viewpoint Video System. This system is designed to transform live sports broadcasts by providing immersive viewing experiences through real-time AI-based video processing at the edge. The technology allows broadcasters to deliver unique angles and near real-time replays, enhancing viewer engagement and enabling new monetization opportunities for live event metrics.

title: Accenture to acquire semiconductor engineering services firm XtremeEDA
summary: Accenture has announced an agreement to acquire XtremeEDA, an Ottawa-based semiconductor engineering services company, for an undisclosed sum. This acquisition will bring XtremeEDA's team of 40 engineers into Accenture Canada, enhancing its edge computing and silicon design capabilities. The move comes amid a global chip shortage and aligns with government efforts to strengthen Canada's semiconductor industry.

title: Chip manufacturers press for federal funding as bill flails in Congress
summary: Semiconductor manufacturing giants Intel and GlobalFoundries are urging Congress to pass the $52 billion CHIPS Act, warning that delays in funding could jeopardize their domestic expansion plans. Intel has already postponed a groundbreaking for its Ohio facility, while GlobalFoundries states its New York plant timeline depends on government investment. The legislation faces hurdles in reconciling differing House and Senate versions, but proponents emphasize its importance for U.S. semiconductor leadership and supply chain resilience.

title: HPE chief Antonio Neri discusses as-a-service, GreenLake’s impact and the critical importance of silicon
summary: HPE CEO Antonio Neri celebrated the company's successful transition to an as-a-service model with its GreenLake platform, fulfilling a 2019 commitment. Neri highlighted GreenLake's growth, now managing over an exabyte of data for 65,000 customers, and emphasized the critical role of silicon in HPE's innovation, including its supercomputers and support for the CHIPS For America Act. He also touched upon the company's stance on societal issues, specifically supporting women's reproductive rights.

title: Xfinity Stream app Launches on Apple TV 4K and Apple TV HD
summary: Comcast has launched the Xfinity Stream app on Apple TV 4K and Apple TV HD, providing Xfinity TV customers with a new way to access their content within their homes. The app introduces a redesigned, intuitive user interface focused on content discovery, including editorial recommendations and personalization. This initiative follows a previous collaboration between Comcast and Apple to bring the Apple TV+ app to Comcast's entertainment platforms.

title: AMD and HPE team up to spur sustainable IT through environmentally friendly initiatives
summary: AMD and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) are collaborating to promote sustainable IT practices by modernizing data centers, focusing on energy efficiency and reducing environmental impact. They have developed supercomputers like the Frontier and LUMI, with LUMI being carbon-negative and powered by renewable energy, demonstrating significant advancements in sustainable computing. The article highlights the growing corporate interest in sustainable IT, driven by institutional investors, customers, and employees.

title: Chip shortage drives up vehicle prices, cuts sales
summary: The global semiconductor shortage severely impacted U.S. new vehicle sales in the second quarter of 2022, leading to a 21% drop compared to the previous year. Despite high gas prices, inflation, and rising interest rates, demand outstripped supply, pushing average new vehicle prices to a record high of nearly $45,000. Automakers like GM and Toyota experienced significant sales declines, and while some expect gradual improvement, the shortage is anticipated to continue frustrating buyers and limiting inventory for the foreseeable future.

title: Direxion SOXL ETF: How To Trade This Leveraged Play On Semis (NYSEARCA:SOXL)
summary: This article discusses the Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares ETF (SOXL), a 3X leveraged ETF on the semiconductor index. Despite SOXL being down 85% this year, the author advises against buying it immediately. The recommendation is to wait for signs of a bottom in the broader semiconductor index (SOXX) before initiating a position in SOXL.

title: Micron Announces Availability of DDR5 DRAM For Next-Gen Intel & AMD Server and Workstation Platforms
summary: Micron has announced the commercial and industrial availability of its DDR5 DRAM specifically designed for next-generation Intel and AMD server and workstation platforms. This new DDR5 memory is expected to dramatically increase system performance by up to 85% over DDR4, catering to demanding AI, HPC, and data-intensive applications. Micron has been a key player in the development of DDR5 specifications and offers support through its Technology Enablement Program.

title: Chip Market: TSMC Sales Soar 44% in Another Sign of Resilient Tech Demand
summary: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) reported better-than-expected quarterly revenue, signaling robust demand in the electronics sector. The world's largest contract chipmaker recorded NT$534.1 billion ($17.9 billion) in revenue for the second quarter, surpassing analyst estimates. This performance indicates that the tech demand is more resilient than previously anticipated, despite broader economic uncertainties.

title: Intel’s Dave Guzzi On The Big Channel Bet, Emerging Technologies And GPU Strategy
summary: Dave Guzzi, General Manager for Systems and Components Partners at Intel, discusses the company's significant commitment to its channel partners, driven by a massive manufacturing investment aimed at shifting chip production away from Asia. He emphasizes the importance of providing partners with secure supply chains and resources like Partner University to help them develop solutions with Intel's emerging technologies, including AI. Guzzi also highlights Intel's ambition to compete in the GPU market, offering partners more choice and leveraging Intel's extensive software development expertise.

title: STMicro and GlobalFoundries to build $5.7B semiconductor fab in France
summary: STMicroelectronics NV and GlobalFoundries Inc. announced plans to construct a new $5.7 billion semiconductor production facility in Crolles, France, with significant financial backing from the French government. This plant, specializing in 300mm wafers and FD-SOI technology, is projected to be fully operational by 2026, aiming for an annual capacity of 620,000 wafers. The initiative seeks to support the growing demand for chips in automotive, IoT, and mobile device sectors, aligning with STMicro's revenue growth goals and GlobalFoundries' 22FDX manufacturing process.

title: Samsung Announces 3nm Process Node, the First with Gate-All-Around FETs
summary: Samsung has announced initial semiconductor production using its 3nm process node, which incorporates Gate-All-Around (GAA) FETs, trademarked as “MBCFET.” This technology marks a significant advancement beyond FinFETs, offering substantial improvements in power consumption, performance, and area reduction. Competitors like Intel and TSMC are also developing their own GAAFET processes, indicating a major shift in transistor architecture to continue Moore's Law.

title: To Infinity and Beyond – New Frontiers for Exascale Supercomputers and DOE National Labs: A Virtual Briefing hosted by TFAI and the National Labs Caucus
summary: This article announces a virtual briefing hosted by TFAI and the National Labs Caucus on July 14, 2022, focusing on the advancements in exascale supercomputing in the U.S. The briefing aims to discuss past breakthroughs, current research, and real-world applications, highlighting the collaboration between the Department of Energy, Oak Ridge National Lab, HPE, and AMD. It features a panel of experts and members of Congress, including Representatives Chuck Fleischmann and Bill Foster.

title: Intel To Hike Chip Prices As Semiconductor Giant Deals With Inflation
summary: Intel has announced price increases across its product lines, including chipsets and microprocessors, by as much as 20 percent, effective in Q4 2022. The company attributes these hikes to inflationary pressures, rising material costs, and increased production expenses. Other companies in the semiconductor industry, including TSMC and various material suppliers, are also implementing price increases, suggesting a broader trend of rising costs in the tech sector.

title: Chip designers warm to U.S. bill despite big benefits to Intel
summary: Major U.S. chip firms are largely supporting a legislative package designed to boost the U.S. computer chip industry, despite earlier concerns that the subsidies disproportionately benefit manufacturers like Intel Corp. The legislation includes $52 billion in subsidies and an investment tax credit, primarily aiding companies that both design and manufacture chips. While chip designers like AMD and Qualcomm initially worried about not receiving direct benefits, they have reportedly agreed not to oppose the bill.

title: Rambus Spins DDR5 DIMM Interface ICs for Data Center Servers
summary: Rambus has unveiled new SPD (serial presence detect) hub and temperature sensor ICs, completing its DDR5 DIMM interface chipset. These new chips, alongside their existing Registering Clock Driver (RCD), provide a comprehensive solution for DDR5 memory modules in PCs and data center servers. The article details the technical advantages of DDR5 over DDR4, emphasizing the role of these new components in optimizing performance, capacity, and thermal management for modern computing demands.

title: Corsair launches 32-inch 4K 144Hz and QHD 240Hz gaming monitors
summary: Corsair has introduced two new high-end 32-inch gaming monitors: the Xeneon 32UHD144 (4K, 144Hz) and the Xeneon 32QHD240 (QHD, 240Hz). Both models feature Quantum Dot IPS panels with extensive color gamut coverage, AMD FreeSync Premium (Nvidia G-Sync compatible), and VESA DisplayHDR 600 certification. They offer flexible purchasing options, allowing customers to save money by opting out of the bundled stand if they plan to use a monitor arm.

title: TSMC: Life’s A Beach, And Then You Make Dies
summary: TSMC reported strong Q2 2022 results with $18.16 billion in revenue and a 44.4% profit margin despite a slowdown in PC and smartphone sales, driven by high-performance computing (HPC) demand. The company is advancing its 3nm and 2nm process nodes, with 3nm on track for volume production in H2 2022 and 2nm for risk production in 2024. The article highlights TSMC's industry leadership compared to Intel's struggles and discusses the implications of the CHIPS Act for domestic chip manufacturing.

title: Micron Ships World’s First 232-Layer NAND, Extends Technology Leadership
summary: Micron Technology has announced it has begun volume production of the world's first 232-layer NAND, marking a significant advancement in storage solutions. This new NAND technology offers industry-leading areal density, unprecedented performance with the fastest I/O speed, and improved energy efficiency, making it suitable for data-intensive applications from client devices to cloud services. The innovation is expected to drive breakthroughs in diverse markets, including data centers, mobile devices, PCs, and artificial intelligence applications.

title: Nomura Malaysia launches Global Shariah Semiconductor Equity Fund
summary: Nomura Asset Management Malaysia Sdn Bhd has launched its Global Shariah Semiconductor Equity Fund to provide investors access to the rapidly expanding semiconductor sector, driven by global digitalisation trends and the rollout of 5G networks. The fund aims to capitalize on the increasing demand for semiconductor chips by focusing on high-growth potential Shariah-compliant companies with strong financials and intellectual property. This fund is the first of its kind in Malaysia, with Nomura Asset Management UK Ltd acting as the investment adviser.

title: Steward Partners Investment Advisory LLC Purchases New Position in ZIM Integrated Shipping Services Ltd. (NYSE:ZIM)
summary: Steward Partners Investment Advisory LLC has acquired a new position in ZIM Integrated Shipping Services Ltd. (NYSE:ZIM) during the first quarter, purchasing 9,268 shares valued at approximately $674,000. Several other institutional investors also adjusted their holdings in ZIM, while analysts have issued varying ratings and price targets for the stock. The article also details ZIM's price performance, financial ratios, recent earnings, and dividend information.

title: Samsung warns of weaker chip demand for phones, PCs as people shop less
summary: Samsung Electronics has issued a warning that chip demand from smartphone and PC manufacturers is expected to decline further as consumer spending decreases. This outlook, shared by the world's leading memory chip and smartphone maker, suggests that even the more resilient demand from server clients might see adjustments due to growing recession fears. While Samsung achieved its best April-June operating profit since 2018 driven by strong server chip demand, its mobile business faced profit weakening amidst geopolitical issues, inflation, and rising costs.

title: Opinion: Which Intel CEO is to blame for the current woes? Or is it actually AMD’s CEO?
summary: This article discusses the blame for Intel's recent disastrous second-quarter results, particularly the significant revenue miss in its data-center business. It explores whether the fault lies with current CEO Pat Gelsinger, his predecessor, or the success of rival AMD CEO Lisa Su. The article highlights that Intel's results were so poor that analysts questioned why the company did not pre-announce them.

title: AMD passes Intel in market cap
summary: AMD's market capitalization surpassed Intel's for the second time, reaching $153 billion compared to Intel's $148 billion. This shift, while largely symbolic, highlights increased competitiveness in the PC and server chip markets and suggests investors may favor asset-light chipmakers. Intel's market cap decline followed disappointing earnings and a reduced full-year forecast, which the company attributed to execution issues and a slowing PC market.

title: Newegg Studios’ “The Gamer Lounge” Challenges Video Game Players in Weekly Episodes
summary: Newegg Commerce, Inc. announced that its reality-competition video series, "The Gamer Lounge," will now release new episodes weekly on YouTube. The show features social media influencers and Newegg hosts competing in various video games, with viewers able to win prizes by predicting episode winners. This move to weekly episodes follows strong viewer interest since its debut in July 2021.

title: UCIe™ (Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express™) Consortium Announces Incorporation and New Board Members; Open for Membership
summary: The UCIe™ (Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express™) Consortium has officially incorporated and announced new board members, including Alibaba and NVIDIA, joining founding members like ASE, AMD, Intel, and Samsung. The consortium aims to advance the UCIe specification to create a universal and interoperable chiplet ecosystem, with the UCIe 1.0 specification already available for download. They are actively seeking new members to contribute to the development of future specifications.

title: Opinion: Take a bow, Lisa Su: AMD’s data-center business is a true rival to Intel
summary: Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has made significant strides in its data-center business, now posing a serious challenge to Intel. Under CEO Lisa Su's leadership, AMD's server efforts have transformed from less than 1% market share in 2018 to reporting $1.5 billion in quarterly revenue, showcasing strong growth while its larger rival struggles. This achievement marks a substantial resurgence for AMD in a critical market segment.

title: How Taiwanese semiconductors reign supreme
summary: Taiwanese semiconductor manufacturer TSMC holds a dominant position in the global microchip market, making the world heavily reliant on its products across various sectors. The company's strategic importance was highlighted during Nancy Pelosi's visit to Taiwan, underscoring geopolitical tensions and a global race for chipmaking dominance. Both the US and Europe are investing heavily to bolster their domestic semiconductor production, while China seeks to gain access to Taiwan's advanced chipmaking technology.

title: Meet Ross Freeman, Inventor of the First FPGA
summary: This article introduces Ross Freeman, the inventor of the first FPGA, detailing his life from a Peace Corps volunteer to a pioneer in modern computing. It highlights his role in founding Xilinx and his radical vision for the FPGA, which transformed the semiconductor industry. The piece also acknowledges his enduring legacy through Xilinx's market dominance and the annual Ross Freeman Award for Technical Innovation.

title: Infineon, AMD Stock Prices Show Diverging Fortunes in Chip Industry
summary: The semiconductor industry is experiencing a divergence in fortunes, with chip manufacturers serving data centers, automotive, and industrial sectors maintaining strong demand, while those in consumer electronics face slowing sales and excess inventory. This trend is evident in the stock performance of companies like Infineon, which saw gains due to automotive demand, and AMD, which fell due to a PC market slump. The article highlights that analysts are drawing a "line in the sand" between these two segments of the chip market, indicating a shift from past blanket statements about the industry.

title: GlobalFoundries, Applied Materials and Ford to Co-Host CEO Summit to Secure U.S. Chip Supply Chain
summary: GlobalFoundries, Applied Materials, and Ford are co-hosting a CEO summit in Washington, D.C. to discuss securing the U.S. chip supply chain following the passage of the CHIPS and Science Act. The summit aims to foster public-private partnerships to accelerate semiconductor manufacturing, support automotive electrification, and strengthen U.S. economy and national security. Business leaders and senior government officials will explore how new investments can advance technology leadership and create jobs in the semiconductor industry.

title: Biden signs bill to boost U.S. semiconductor chip production, compete with China
summary: President Biden signed a landmark bill providing $52.7 billion in subsidies for U.S. semiconductor production and research, aiming to strengthen domestic manufacturing and enhance competitiveness with China. The legislation, which includes a 25% investment tax credit for chip plants, seeks to address persistent chip shortages affecting various industries and boost U.S. scientific research. Despite some criticism regarding aid to profitable companies, the White House highlighted new investments spurred by the bill from companies like Qualcomm and Micron.

title: AMD Stock Is a Bargain
summary: Morningstar considers AMD stock a bargain with a fair value estimate of $130 per share, giving it a 4-star rating and a narrow economic moat. The company is praised for its impressive growth, market share gains against Intel, and strong execution in new chip designs, especially in the data center and PC markets. However, high uncertainty remains due to the cyclical semiconductor industry and AMD's smaller scale compared to Intel and Nvidia.

title: Beelink SER5 Review – An AMD Ryzen 5 5600H mini PC tested with Windows 11, Ubuntu 22.04
summary: This article reviews the Beelink SER5 mini PC, equipped with an AMD Ryzen 5 5600H processor, 32GB DDR4 RAM, and a 500GB NVMe SSD. The author details the hardware, installation processes, and performance benchmarks for both Windows 11 and Ubuntu 22.04. The review concludes that while the SER5 offers very good performance, it should be considered an alternative to the SER4 rather than a significant upgrade due to similar performance metrics, with Ubuntu often outperforming Windows in gaming.

title: Thematic Times: Semiconductors Power Thematic Indexes
summary: The article discusses how semiconductor technology drives many thematic investment indexes, particularly after the increased recognition of their importance due to pandemic-related supply issues. It highlights the impact of the newly signed CHIPS and Science Act, which provides significant subsidies to boost domestic chip manufacturing. The piece also explains how various thematic indexes, such as those for electric vehicles, streaming, gaming, and even space technology, are directly or indirectly exposed to the semiconductor industry and stand to benefit from these developments.

title: Thematic Times: Semiconductors Power Thematic Indexes
summary: The article discusses how semiconductor stocks are crucial to many thematic indexes, particularly after the CHIPS and Science Act. It highlights direct and indirect exposure of various thematic indexes to semiconductors and the long-term benefits of the CHIPS Act for the industry and end users. The growth of the semiconductor market is primarily driven by the automotive sector, data storage, and wireless communications.

title: Desktop CPU Sales Lowest in 30 Years, AMD Gains Market Share Anyway (Updated)
summary: Desktop CPU shipments hit a nearly 30-year low in Q2 2022, marking the largest year-over-year decline in Mercury Research's records. Despite the overall market slump, AMD significantly increased its market share and revenue in desktop, mobile, and server segments, setting new records for x86 processor share and mobile unit share. Intel, in contrast, reported a rare loss and delayed key products, indicating a challenging period for the long-standing leader.

title: Newegg Rolls into CNE Gaming Garage Powered by AMD with Advanced Battlestations Gaming PC
summary: Newegg announced its participation in the CNE Gaming Garage Powered by AMD in Toronto, where it will display a custom Advanced Battlestations (ABS) gaming PC. Attendees can enter a sweepstakes to win the high-value PC, built by Newegg's Buildeniac division and featuring AMD and ASUS components. Newegg Canada staff will also be on-site during the opening weekend to engage with attendees and discuss AMD Advantage series laptops.

title: Report: Qualcomm plans to return to the server chip market
summary: Qualcomm Inc. is reportedly planning to re-enter the server chip market four years after exiting it, seeking customers for a new server processor. This new processor is based on technology acquired from Nuvia Inc. for $1.4 billion and is being evaluated by Amazon Web Services Inc. The move aligns with Qualcomm's strategy to expand beyond mobile and boost revenue, potentially increasing competition for Intel Corp. and Advanced Micro Devices Inc.

title: Intel shares new details about upcoming Meteor Lake processor series
summary: Intel has revealed new technical details about its upcoming Meteor Lake processor series, which will utilize chiplet technology for desktop and laptop CPUs. These processors will feature five chiplets, with the primary CPU module manufactured by Intel and other vital components like the GPU and I/O module produced by TSMC. This chiplet architecture allows for greater flexibility in design, improved manufacturing efficiency, and better performance through 3D stacking with Intel's Foveros technology.

title: Making AI Run At Any Scale But Not At All Costs
summary: This article discusses the challenges and solutions for scaling AI applications, highlighting how companies like HPE and Lenovo are developing accessible AI products. It focuses on Anyscale, a company founded by UC Berkeley researchers, which commercializes the open-source Ray framework to simplify distributed AI computing and make it easier for enterprises to develop and deploy AI models without extensive infrastructure expertise.

title: Why Supermicro for On-Prem Data Centers
summary: Supermicro offers compelling advantages for on-premise data centers, enabling organizations to deploy the latest CPU and GPU technologies quickly due to Supermicro's close relationships with suppliers. The company provides a broad range of application and workload-optimized server and storage configurations, including liquid cooling options, to meet specific performance requirements and reduce TCO. Supermicro also offers comprehensive IT solutions, from single nodes to large clusters, with full assembly, testing, and rack integration services to ensure optimal performance and customer satisfaction.

title: AMD Set To Become TSMC’s Second Largest 5nm Customer Courtesy Of Ryzen 7000
summary: AMD is projected to become TSMC's second-largest customer for 5nm technology, primarily driven by its upcoming Ryzen 7000 series processors. These new CPUs, including desktop, notebook, and datacenter variants, will extensively utilize TSMC's 5nm process node. This increased demand will solidify AMD's position as a major client for the Taiwanese chip manufacturer, second only to Apple.

title: AMD Launches Ryzen 7000 Series Desktop Processors with “Zen 4” Architecture: the Fastest Core in Gaming¹
summary: AMD has officially launched its Ryzen 7000 Series Desktop processors, featuring the new "Zen 4" architecture. These processors, built on a 5nm process, offer significant performance improvements for gaming and content creation, including up to 16 cores and 32 threads. Alongside the new CPUs, AMD introduced the Socket AM5 platform, which supports DDR5 memory and PCIe 5.0, and unveiled AMD EXPO™ technology for advanced DDR5 memory overclocking.

title: AMD launching what it claims is ‘fastest processer in the world for gaming’ in September
summary: AMD announced the launch of its new Ryzen 7000 series gaming processors, touted as the "fastest processor in the world for gaming" by CEO Lisa Su. The processors are based on a 5-nanometer Zen 4 core architecture. This release comes amidst a challenging period for PC sales, with the low-end Ryzen 5 7600x model priced at $299.

title: How to Get Windows 11 on HP Computers
summary: This article provides a comprehensive guide on how to upgrade HP computers to Windows 11. It covers the benefits of upgrading, the essential system requirements, and step-by-step instructions for installation via Windows Update or the Windows 11 Installation Assistant. Additionally, it offers advice on preparing the computer for installation, backing up files, and troubleshooting common issues after the upgrade.

title: U.S. Restricts Sales of Sophisticated Chips to China and Russia
summary: The Biden administration has imposed new restrictions on the sale of sophisticated computer chips, specifically high-end graphics processing units (GPUs), to China and Russia. This move, affecting companies like Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices, aims to prevent These chips, crucial for supercomputers and artificial intelligence used in applications like weapons development and intelligence gathering, from being used for military purposes. Nvidia estimates this restriction will impact approximately $400 million in revenue.

title: Rising Contract Pricing Offsets Falling Demand, Total 2Q22 NAND Flash Revenue Increases 1.1% QoQ, Says TrendForce
summary: Despite sluggish consumer demand and a decrease in bit shipments, the total NAND Flash industry revenue rose by 1.1% QoQ in 2Q22 to US$18.12 billion, primarily driven by a 3-8% increase in contract pricing due to a Kioxia raw material contamination incident. Enterprise SSD purchasing demonstrated strong momentum, offsetting weaker demand in consumer segments. However, TrendForce predicts a challenging 3Q22 with expected price drops of 13-18% and an overall revenue decline of 10% QoQ due to oversupply and aggressive destocking.

title: U.S. officials order Nvidia to halt sales of top AI chips to China
summary: U.S. officials have ordered Nvidia to cease exporting its top AI chips, the A100 and H100, to China, significantly escalating the U.S. crackdown on China's technological advancements. This move, aimed at preventing the chips from being used for military purposes, could hinder Chinese firms' ability to perform advanced AI work and impact Nvidia's future sales in the region. Rival AMD also received similar restrictions on its MI250 chips, but stated the impact on its business would not be material.

title: New U.S. curbs on sales of Nvidia AI chips to China spark selloff
summary: New U.S. restrictions on exports of advanced AI chips from Nvidia and AMD to China have caused Nvidia's stock to fall over 11%. The curbs, aimed at limiting China's technological prowess, specifically target Nvidia's H100 and A100 chips which are crucial for AI and machine learning applications. While potentially impacting Nvidia's business in China, the U.S. government has made some allowances for the completion and support of the H100 chip.

title: Taiwan chip magnate pledges cash for defense against China: 'I'm telling everyone to oppose the CCP'
summary: Robert Tsao, founder of Taiwan's first semiconductor company UMC, has pledged $32 million of his personal wealth to fund the Kuma Academy, which trains Taiwan's civilian defense corps. This initiative aims to train over three million "civilian warriors" and sharpshooters annually to defend against a potential Chinese invasion. Tsao, who has regained Taiwanese citizenship after giving it up, stated his strong opposition to the Chinese Communist Party, a stance he adopted after witnessing the crackdown on pro-democracy supporters in Hong Kong.

title: Skyworks (SWKS) Unveils Network Synchronization Solutions
summary: Skyworks Solutions (SWKS) has launched new network synchronization solutions, including Si551x and Si540x products and AccuTime IEEE 1588 software, to support 5G fronthaul networks with precise time synchronization. These solutions, developed in partnership with NXP Semiconductors and Advanced Micro Devices, are crucial for emerging 5G applications and the IoT. Despite a challenging macroeconomic environment and competition, Skyworks aims to leverage organic growth and an expanding product portfolio to capitalize on 5G and IoT megatrends.

title: Meta, Qualcomm sign pact on custom virtual reality chips | Hindustan Times
summary: Meta Platforms Inc. and Qualcomm Inc. have signed an agreement for Qualcomm to produce custom chipsets for Meta's Quest virtual reality (VR) devices. This collaboration highlights Meta's reliance on Qualcomm's technology while it also develops its own custom silicon for its VR, augmented, and mixed reality devices. The financial terms of the deal were not disclosed, and the custom chipsets will be optimized for Quest's system specifications, though not exclusive to Meta.

title: Game on: Why esports and gaming could take it to the next level
summary: The esports and gaming sector, a multi-billion-dollar industry, continues to grow despite recent slowdowns attributed to supply chain delays and shifting consumer choices post-pandemic. Analysts and industry experts are confident in long-term growth, citing upcoming title launches and easing parts shortages. Mobile gaming remains the primary driver of revenue growth globally, while esports is gaining mainstream traction with significant investment and increasing popularity in emerging markets.

title: Chip Delivery Times Shrink in August
summary: Global chip delivery times shortened in August to an average of 26.8 weeks, a slight improvement signifying easing supply shortages due to slowing demand for products like phones and PCs. Despite this, certain semiconductors such as power management and microcontrollers remain hard to acquire, and the industry struggles to match supply with demand, leading to concerns about over-ordering and inventory build-up. The Philadelphia Stock Market Semiconductor Index has seen a significant downturn, reflecting investor apprehension.

title: Pentagon eyes commercial solution to supply chain problems
summary: The U.S. Department of Defense is seeking commercial software solutions to address ongoing supply chain disruptions, which have been exacerbated by the pandemic and inflation. The Defense Innovation Unit (DIU) issued a solicitation for software that can build new supply pathways and manage risk for critical components, especially important given concerns about foreign reliance for microelectronics and recent issues like the F-35's Chinese-made magnet. The Pentagon aims to use analytical techniques to integrate data from various sources, create supply chain models, estimate costs, and identify risks.

title: US-China chip war is overheating
summary: The Biden administration plans to expand restrictions on U.S. semiconductor and chipmaking tool exports to China, targeting advanced AI chips and manufacturing equipment. These new regulations will codify earlier limitations imposed on companies like KLA Corp, Lam Research Corp, Applied Materials Inc, Nvidia Corp, and Advanced Micro Devices. The move is part of the U.S. strategy to curb China's technological advancements and strengthen its domestic semiconductor industry through initiatives like the $50 billion CHIPS Act.

title: Supermicro Adds New 8U Universal GPU Server That Delivers Maximum Performance and Flexibility for Large Scale AI Training, NVIDIA® Omniverse, and Metaverse
summary: Supermicro has launched a new 8U Universal GPU server designed for large-scale AI training, NVIDIA Omniverse, and the Metaverse. This server features eight NVIDIA H100 Tensor Core GPUs, offering maximum performance and flexibility. Its advanced airflow design helps reduce Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) in data centers while maintaining high performance.

title: Supermicro Announces 8U H100 HGX Server
summary: Supermicro has announced its new 8U Universal GPU Server, incorporating eight NVIDIA H100 Tensor Core GPUs, designed for large-scale AI training, NVIDIA Omniverse, and Metaverse applications. This server increases AI performance with improved thermal density, enabling lower power consumption and operation at higher data center temperatures, while supporting both current and next-generation CPUs and GPUs. The company is expanding its leading GPU server lineup with this addition, offering flexibility with front and rear I/O, AC or DC power support, and liquid cooling options.

title: Arm unveils updated Neoverse CPU roadmap, targeting cloud, hyperscale and HPC workloads
summary: Arm has announced an updated Neoverse CPU roadmap to provide a foundation for various workloads in cloud, hyperscale, and high-performance computing. The new Neoverse V2, codenamed Demeter, aims to deliver top per-thread performance and power efficiency, with partners like Nvidia already integrating it. Additionally, Arm detailed upcoming N-series and E-series chip designs set for a 2023 launch, catering to different compute needs prioritizing thread count or data plane processing.

title: Google and NIST Partner to Open Source Nanotechnology Testbed Wafers
summary: Google and the US National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) have partnered to create an open-source, wafer-scale testbed for nanotechnology research, built on SkyWater Technologies' SKY130 process node. Google will distribute these nanotech test wafers to universities and release the designs under an open-source license. This initiative aims to facilitate research, improve access to semiconductor technology, and streamline the transition of innovations from lab to practical application.

title: Data, Data everywhere, but where to store it all
summary: The world is generating an unprecedented amount of data, leading to concerns about running out of storage space. Current trends show data creation outpacing storage capacity, with Zettabytes of information being generated daily. The article explores technological advancements in hard drive capacities, such as HAMR and MAMR, and radical concepts like quantum computing and graphene storage, while also highlighting the practical challenges of implementing these solutions and the long-term environmental impact of data storage.

title: Serverless Data Centers – Wave of the future?
summary: This article explores the concept of "serverless data centers" by combining serverless computing and hardware disaggregation. It explains how serverless computing allows developers to focus on application code by abstracting infrastructure management, while hardware disaggregation breaks down monolithic servers into pools of specialized resources. The author argues that true serverless data centers, leveraging disaggregated hardware, will enable flexible and efficient resource allocation tailored precisely to application needs.

title: NVIDIA Delivers Quantum Leap in Performance, Introduces New Era of Neural Rendering With GeForce RTX 40 Series
summary: NVIDIA unveiled its new GeForce RTX 40 Series GPUs, powered by the Ada Lovelace architecture and new DLSS 3 technology, promising up to 4x performance improvement for gamers and creators. The flagship RTX 4090 and two configurations of the RTX 4080 were introduced, alongside new features like neural rendering and RTX Remix for classic games. These advancements aim to redefine real-time ray tracing, AI-powered graphics, and creative workflows.

title: Nvidia’s new AI-focused chip produces more realistic images in games
summary: Nvidia Corp. has unveiled its new AI-focused graphics chip, the RTX 4090, which is designed to produce more realistic images in games by combining AI with traditional pixel calculations. The high-end chip, costing US$1,599, features 76 billion transistors and 24GB of memory, and will go on sale October 12, with other versions following in November. This launch aims to give customers a reason to upgrade amidst a slowdown in PC component demand.

title: Supermicro Delivers Second-Generation NVIDIA® OVX™ Computing System for 3D Collaboration, Metaverse, and Digital Twin Simulation, Powered by the New NVIDIA L40 GPU
summary: Supermicro announced the release of its second-generation NVIDIA OVX computing system, powered by the new NVIDIA L40 GPU, designed for 3D collaboration, metaverse, and digital twin simulation. These new servers aim to provide the necessary graphics, AI, and network performance for building and operating immersive 3D virtual worlds at data center scale. The systems will support up to eight NVIDIA L40 GPUs, ConnectX-7 SmartNICs, and current/future CPU processors from Intel and AMD.

title: 7 Meme Stocks to Buy During a Stock Market Crash
summary: This article identifies seven meme stocks that aggressive investors might consider buying during a stock market crash for long-term potential. The selection includes companies like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), FedEx (FDX), GameStop (GME), NVIDIA (NVDA), Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), and Tesla (TSLA). Each stock is discussed with reasons for its meme stock appeal and potential for recovery despite current market downturns.

title: STMicroelectronics Stellar P6 MCUs Targeted at Next-gen EV Platforms
summary: STMicroelectronics has introduced its new Stellar P6 automotive MCUs, designed for next-generation electric vehicles (EVs). These MCUs are the first qualifiable devices for model year 2024 to integrate the CAN-XL in-car communication standard, enabling efficient handling of increasing data flows in EVs. The Stellar P series combines advanced actuation capabilities with powerful function integration, specifically targeting new drivetrain trends and domain-oriented architectures for optimal real-time performance and energy management in electric vehicles.

title: Intel Unveils New Chips and Software as It Chases a Comeback (INTC)
summary: Intel Corp. introduced new personal-computer processors, graphics semiconductors, and software as part of its strategy to regain its position in the chip industry. The new lineup includes Core desktop processors for high-performance users and a graphics chip named Ponte Vecchio for data centers, aimed at challenging Nvidia. These announcements were made at Intel's Innovate event, showcasing their efforts towards a comeback.

title: Micron’s big new Boise fab will slow US economy, bring higher taxes and worse
summary: Economist Peter Crabb argues that Micron Technology's new $15 billion Boise semiconductor plant, despite being seen as an economic boon, will likely slow the U.S. economy and lead to higher taxes due to federal subsidies and government borrowing. He also suggests that Micron's shareholders will face higher costs and a lower return on investment, as indicated by the company's stock performance and low price-to-earnings ratio compared to peers. Crabb emphasizes the often-overlooked long-term economic consequences of such large-scale, government-subsidized projects.

title: Meet the Harvard Law School graduate helping China cut its reliance on US AI chips
summary: Michael Zhang Wen, a Harvard Law School graduate and chairman of Biren Technology, is leading China's efforts to reduce its dependence on US artificial intelligence (AI) chips. His Shanghai-based start-up recently released the BR100, a 7-nanometre GPU, which some believe could position Biren to become "China's Nvidia" amidst escalating US export restrictions on advanced chips. Zhang faces pressure to deliver, as his company has raised a substantial US$655 million from major venture capital funds.

title: SoftBank’s Son expected to meet Samsung's Lee for Arm M&A discussion
summary: SoftBank Group CEO Masayoshi Son is anticipated to meet with Samsung Electronics de facto leader Jay Y. Lee to discuss a potential sale or strategic alliance for British chip designer Arm. This meeting follows a collapsed deal to sell Arm to Nvidia due to regulatory hurdles and a broader industry consensus that a single company acquisition might be difficult. Various scenarios are being considered, including a consortium of chipmakers or a partial acquisition by Samsung, though some industry watchers express skepticism about significant outcomes given Arm's high valuation and SoftBank's financial state.

title: TSMC remains world’s largest contract chipmaker
summary: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (TSMC) maintained its position as the world's largest contract chipmaker in Q2, holding a 53.4% market share despite a slight dip from the previous quarter. The company recorded $18.15 billion in sales due to demand from high-performance computing, IoT, and automotive electronics. South Korea's Samsung Electronics and Taiwan's United Microelectronics Corp followed with significant market shares.

title: Meta launches AI software tools to ease switching between Nvidia, AMD chips
summary: Meta Platforms Inc. has launched new open-source AI software tools to enable easier switching between different underlying chips from Nvidia and AMD. These tools, based on PyTorch, can significantly speed up code execution on both Nvidia A100 and AMD MI250 chips, offering developers greater flexibility and reducing migration costs between hardware vendors. This initiative aims to address the vendor lock-in common with Nvidia's CUDA platform and improve hardware choice for deep learning developers.

title: Intel’s Journey From 100 Billion To 1 Trillion Transistors Was Painted At The IntelON 2022 Event
summary: During the IntelON 2022 event, CEO Pat Gelsinger outlined Intel's ambitious goal to increase transistor count from 100 billion to 1 trillion by the end of the decade, reinforcing Moore's Law. The company also announced significant advancements, including new data center and gaming GPUs (Flex Series, Ponte Vecchio, Arc A770), and the 13th Gen Core Processors (Raptor Lake). Intel's focus remains on an end-to-end foundry approach, expansion of the UCIe consortium for open chiplet ecosystems, and strengthening its offerings in both consumer and enterprise markets.

title: Ethereum Merge Not Yet Boosting Crypto Stocks
summary: The Ethereum merge has not yet boosted crypto-correlated stocks, including holdings in ETFs like SATO and BLKC, partly because most public crypto miners focus on Bitcoin. However, the switch to staking could benefit companies like Coinbase through increased revenue from staking services, potentially offering longer-term upsides for crypto equities and related ETFs. The article notes that ETH mining equipment is cheaper and more flexible than BTC mining equipment, using GPUs, which could benefit companies like Nvidia and AMD.

title: The Chips That Make Taiwan the Center of the World
summary: This article examines Taiwan's critical role in the global semiconductor industry, particularly through TSMC, and the geopolitical risks associated with its dependence on the island amid rising U.S.-China tensions. It explores the potential catastrophic economic consequences for the world if military action or political pressure disrupts Taiwan's chip manufacturing capabilities. The author argues that while Taiwan's chip industry provides a "silicon shield," it also makes the island a crucial target in any conflict.

title: MICLEDI Microdisplays and Kura Technologies Announce Partnership on Custom Micro-LED Display for Ultra High-Performance AR glasses, Scaling with Partners Including GlobalFoundries and TSMC
summary: MICLEDI Microdisplays and Kura Technologies have partnered to develop custom micro-LED displays for high-performance AR glasses. Kura's next-generation AR glasses will incorporate MICLEDI's 300mm microLED displays, with both companies leveraging expertise from partners like GlobalFoundries and TSMC for scaled production. This collaboration aims to bring advanced 8K AR glasses to market sooner by overcoming typical manufacturing challenges.

title: IBM to invest $20 billion in New York's Hudson Valley region over 10 years
summary: IBM announced plans to invest $20 billion in New York's Hudson Valley region over the next 10 years, coinciding with a visit from President Joe Biden to its Poughkeepsie site. This investment aims to boost the technology ecosystem in New York, focusing on semiconductors, computers, hybrid cloud, artificial intelligence, and quantum computing. IBM anticipates direct benefits from the CHIPS and Science Act, ensuring a stable supply of next-generation chips.

title: AMD Chief Shares Her Secret Sauce Behind Successful Zen CPUs & Company Turnaround
summary: Dr. Lisa Su, CEO of AMD, discussed the company's performance, industry trends, and the importance of employee productivity in an interview. She highlighted her focus on product development, analyzing market inflection points, and strong customer relationships as key to AMD's turnaround. Dr. Su also emphasized "extreme communication" and the need for product differentiation in a competitive industry, believing the high-performance and adaptive computing markets will see increased demand.

title: Big data helps supercharge materials discovery
summary: Toyota is leveraging big data and machine learning through its Wavebase project to accelerate materials discovery, particularly for automotive components like catalysts, batteries, and magnets. This approach aims to reduce reliance on rare metals, cut costs, and improve performance by systematically analyzing material data and predicting new material candidates. The Wavebase system, which uses sophisticated algorithms and visualization tools, is also being extended to benefit the broader Japanese industry and enable data sharing for collaborative research.

title: U.S. aims to hobble China’s chip industry with sweeping new export rules
summary: The Biden administration has enacted sweeping new export controls designed to significantly impede China's semiconductor industry. These rules aim to cut China off from certain chips made with U.S. tools globally and restrict the sale of advanced chip-making equipment, potentially setting China's chip manufacturing back by years. The U.S. acknowledges that the effectiveness of these unilateral controls depends on allied nations adopting similar measures.

title: 10 Best Laptops For Data Science And Data Analysis
summary: This article identifies the key specifications required for laptops used in data science and data analysis, such as ample RAM, fast processors, SSD storage, and CUDA-capable graphics cards. It then lists and describes 10 HP laptop models, including the HP ENVY 17t, HP Pavilion, and HP ZBook 15 G5, highlighting their suitability for various data-intensive tasks and user budgets. The guide emphasizes that while cloud solutions are common, local machine power remains crucial for data professionals.

title: U.S. aims to hobble China's chip industry with sweeping new export rules
summary: The Biden administration has enacted broad new export controls to impede China's chip industry, specifically targeting its access to advanced semiconductors made with U.S. equipment. These measures expand prior restrictions and are designed to slow Beijing's technological and military advancements, although their effectiveness relies on international cooperation. The rules are expected to significantly impact China's chip manufacturing capabilities, potentially setting the industry back years.

title: Chip stocks sink to lowest since 2020 as U.S. expands China curbs
summary: Shares of semiconductor companies experienced a significant decline globally, hitting their lowest levels since 2020, following new U.S. restrictions on China's access to American technology. This sell-off was exacerbated by a disappointing start to the earnings season, intensifying concerns about an industry downturn. The measures include curbs on chips used in AI and supercomputing, and tighter rules on semiconductor equipment sales to Chinese entities, prompting fears of retaliation from Beijing and further negative impacts on the global semiconductor sector.

title: AMD CEO Lisa Su doesn't think U.S. chip export controls will hurt her company in the short term
summary: AMD CEO Lisa Su believes that new U.S. export controls on chips to China will not significantly harm her company's business in the short term, despite China accounting for 25% of AMD's revenue. She emphasizes a long-term belief in increased technology demand and the necessity of navigating geopolitical challenges. Su also highlights the importance of supply chain diversity, citing the CHIPS and Science Act as beneficial for U.S. manufacturing.

title: Intel Plans to Lay Off Thousands of Employees As the Chipmaker Looks to Trim Costs
summary: Intel Corp. is preparing for significant cost-cutting layoffs, potentially affecting thousands of employees, in response to a struggling personal-computer market and declining demand for its processors. The reductions are expected to be announced around the time of its third-quarter earnings report on October 27, with some divisions facing cuts of up to 20% of staff. This move reflects Intel's efforts to trim expenses, improve profit margins, and regain its competitive edge amidst a broader tech spending downturn.

title: Heterogeneous Integration Co-Design Won’t Be Easy
summary: Heterogeneous integration, which involves combining smaller chiplets into a system-in-package (SiP), is becoming crucial for advanced silicon chip design, reducing dependence on Moore's Law. This approach requires seamless co-design and collaboration across engineering teams, utilizing advanced EDA tools for electrical, thermal, and mechanical analysis. Successful implementation hinges on optimizing chip design, process, and packaging together, with the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) standard facilitating this complex integration.

title: TSMC cuts capex on tool delays, demand woes; cautious on outlook
summary: TSMC, the world's largest contract chipmaker, has cut its 2022 capital expenditure by at least 10% and issued a cautious outlook for demand, predicting a chip downturn next year due to rising inflation and other macroeconomic factors. Despite an 80% surge in third-quarter profit, the company expects the semiconductor industry to decline in 2023, though TSMC itself anticipates a growth year. The company cited tool delivery challenges and capacity optimization as reasons for the capex reduction and stated that new U.S. export controls on advanced chip manufacturing for China would have a limited impact on its operations.

title: Cadence, Google Cloud team on system, semiconductor design ...
summary: Cadence has partnered with Google Cloud to accelerate system and semiconductor design using cloud-ready tools, resulting in up to a 10x improvement in design and verification throughput. Cadence tools, including the Cloud Passport, have been certified for Google Cloud, with Google silicon teams already utilizing them for developing Tensor SoCs and TPUs. This collaboration aims to provide engineers with enhanced scalability, access to state-of-the-art HPC infrastructure, and faster time to solution.

title: The Sweeping Impact of New U.S. Semiconductor Restrictions
summary: New U.S. restrictions on semiconductor sales, chipmaking equipment, and supercomputer parts are significantly impacting China's chip industry, causing a potential brain drain as American personnel face tough choices. While these policies aim to curb China's technological ambitions, they are also a "double-edged sword" for U.S. companies that rely on the Chinese market. Experts suggest these measures could accelerate China's push for self-sufficiency in semiconductors and shift focus towards mature chip technologies.

title: Samsung, Qualcomm unveil industry’s fastest LPDDR5X DRAM chip
summary: Samsung Electronics Co. has successfully demonstrated the performance of its LPDDR5X DRAM on Qualcomm’s Snapdragon mobile platform, achieving an industry-leading speed of 8.5 gigabits per second (Gbps). This advancement surpasses Samsung's previous record and solidifies its leadership in the memory market. The new high-speed, low-power memory is expected to enhance mobile, gaming, camera, and AI applications and find broader use in PCs, HPC, servers, and autonomous driving.

title: ASML reports strong Q3, unaffected by U.S. China sanctions
summary: ASML Holding NV posted better-than-expected third-quarter sales and profit, along with record new bookings of 8.9 billion euros. The company, a crucial equipment supplier for chip manufacturers like TSMC, Samsung, and Intel, stated that it anticipates no significant impact from U.S. sanctions on China. Despite global macroeconomic concerns and weakness in memory chips, ASML's demand remains strong, with a backlog exceeding 30 billion euros, prompting plans to expand production capacity by 2025.

title: The 5x Spike & Now Implosion of Generac’s Stock Shows What Went Horribly Wrong in the Markets since Feb. 2020
summary: This article by Wolf Richter on Wolf Street examines the implosion of Generac Holdings (GNRC) stock, which surged five-fold from February 2020 to November 2021 before dropping 79% from its peak. Richter uses Generac as a case study to demonstrate the "stock-market craziness" fueled by factors like ZIRP and QE policies, and the subsequent brutal unwinding of these inflated valuations. The piece also criticizes Wall Street analysts for maintaining "buy" ratings even after significant stock drops, and discusses the broader implications for dip-buyers and the market as a whole.

title: KIOXIA's next-gen EDSFF E1.S SSDs for hyperscale data centers released
summary: KIOXIA has unveiled its new XD7P Series SSDs, which are designed for hyperscale and general server applications using the EDSFF E1.S form factor. These second-generation E1.S SSDs come with improved performance, including up to double the sequential write and random read/write performance compared to their predecessors. While initially available in PCIe 4.0 variants, KIOXIA has PCIe 5.0 versions under development, to be released based on customer demand.

title: Why Are Semiconductor Stocks Getting Crushed?
summary: Semiconductor stocks have experienced significant losses in 2022 due to growing recession fears, oversupply from pandemic-driven demand, and new strict U.S. export restrictions on sales to China. These restrictions, particularly impacting high-performance chips and manufacturing equipment, are expected to cost several firms hundreds of millions to billions in revenue. While the near-term outlook is dim, the long-term growth expectation for the semiconductor market remains intact, despite anticipated cyclical downturns.

title: US weighs China tech restrictions on quantum computing and AI
summary: The Biden administration is considering new export controls to limit China's access to emerging computing technologies like quantum computing and artificial intelligence. These potential restrictions follow earlier measures aimed at curbing China's use of advanced semiconductors for military and surveillance purposes. The move risks further escalating tensions with China and forcing other nations to choose between the two economic powers.

title: China’s Biren GPUs Can’t Be Made at TSMC Due To US Regulations & Competition With NVIDIA
summary: Birentech, a Chinese chip startup, is reportedly barred from manufacturing its Biren GPUs at TSMC due to US regulations aimed at restricting China's access to advanced AI and datacenter chips. This decision is influenced by the Biren BR100's potential to outperform NVIDIA's A100 chips, which are already banned for the Chinese market, and could severely impact Birentech's manufacturing plans.

title: Walmart sets the stage for crypto payments
summary: Walmart is moving towards adopting cryptocurrencies as a payment option for its customers and plans to create NFTs in the metaverse, according to its Global Chief of Technology. This move could significantly bridge the gap between cryptocurrencies and mainstream consumers. The article also touches on other crypto news, including institutional adoption by Fidelity, legal issues with NFT project Slotie, Solana's continued blockchain outages, new crypto regulations in the UK, and Kraken's compliance with EU sanctions against Russia.

title: Samsung Elec to expand foundry business to tackle TSMC
summary: Samsung Electronics plans to significantly expand its foundry business, particularly focusing on mature and specialty nodes, to challenge TSMC's dominance. The South Korean tech giant aims to more than double its production capacity for these chip types by 2027, targeting areas like automotive and consumer electronics where TSMC currently holds over 50% of the market share. This strategic shift comes as Samsung looks to increase its overall market share in the foundry sector, where it has historically lagged behind TSMC despite advancements in leading-edge technologies.

title: MKS Instruments Maintains Quarterly Dividend at $0.22 Per Share; Payable Dec. 9 to Shareholders of Record on Nov. 28
summary: MKS Instruments has announced that it will maintain its quarterly dividend at $0.22 per share. The dividend is set to be payable on December 9 to shareholders of record as of November 28. This decision reflects the company's continued commitment to returning value to its shareholders.

title: Why Are Semiconductor Shares Being Crushed?
summary: Semiconductor stocks are facing significant declines in 2022 due to multiple factors, including recession fears, weak PC sales, and strict US export restrictions on sales to China. These restrictions are expected to heavily impact major companies like Nvidia and AMD, although the long-term growth outlook for the overall semiconductor market remains positive, albeit with near-term uncertainties.

title: How to design wireless charging systems for safe, reliable operation
summary: This article details how to design wireless charging systems for safe and reliable operation, addressing challenges like space limitations, robust protection from ESD and overloads, and power efficiency. It provides recommendations for circuit protection, efficient control, and safety sensing for power adapters, charging cables, and charging pads, ensuring compliance with international safety standards like Qi and USB protocols. The article also emphasizes the importance of early engagement with manufacturers' application engineers to streamline the design process.

title: Mobileye pops more than 37% in market debut after spinning out of Intel
summary: Mobileye, Intel's self-driving car technology company, saw its shares surge over 37% in its stock market debut after spinning out of Intel. The IPO, which valued Mobileye at around $17 billion, raised $861 million, with Intel planning to use some proceeds to build chip factories. This market debut provides a notable tech IPO in a slow year, despite Mobileye's valuation being below Intel's initial expectations.

title: AMD Issues Performance Advisory for Ryzen 7000 Processors
summary: AMD has released a performance advisory for its new Ryzen 7000 processors, acknowledging reports of unexpected performance variations in certain games. The company is actively investigating these issues and plans to collaborate with game developers and hardware partners for optimization. AMD also addressed concerns about Windows 11 performance, stating that their testing showed no significant differences compared to Windows 10.

title: Arm Could Change Licensing Model to Charge OEMs Directly
summary: Arm might change its licensing model by requiring OEMs to license almost all of its IP if they wish to use its CPU IP, directly challenging existing deals and potentially pushing chipmakers toward alternatives like RISC-V. This potential shift, revealed through Qualcomm's counterclaim in a legal dispute, would prevent mixing Arm's IP with other companies' designs (e.g., AMD GPUs in Samsung chips). The change is expected to take effect in 2025, with exemptions for long-term licensees like NVIDIA and Apple.

title: Semiconductors: Three-way Contest In 2022, Plus Predictions For 2023 And Beyond
summary: The semiconductor industry saw a three-way contest in 2022, with TSMC topping the third quarter, while Intel is expected to finish a distant third for the full year. Although a chip downturn is predicted for 2023, growth is expected to return in 2024, with the industry potentially reaching $1 trillion in sales by 2032. The author emphasizes the importance of India approving silicon fab proposals to capitalize on the upcoming upcycle in the middle of the decade.

title: Vishay buys SiC MOSFET firm for $50m
summary: Vishay Intertechnology has acquired MaxPower Semiconductor, a silicon carbide MOSFET supplier, for $50 million. MaxPower's IP portfolio, including over 100 patents and proprietary device structures for SiC MOSFETs, will be integrated into Vishay’s MOSFET segment to support high-voltage electrification applications. This strategic acquisition aims to expand Vishay's product portfolio and strengthen its competitive position in fast-growing markets like electric vehicles.

title: Micron Ships World's Most Advanced DRAM Technology With 1-Beta Node
summary: Micron Technology has announced it is shipping qualification samples of its 1β (1-beta) DRAM technology to select smartphone manufacturers and chipset partners, achieving mass production readiness for the world's most advanced DRAM technology node. This LPDDR5X mobile memory delivers top speeds of 8.5 gigabits per second, with significant gains in power efficiency (15% improvement) and bit density (over 35% improvement) at 16Gb per die capacity. The 1β node is poised to support highly responsive applications from mobile to data centers and intelligent vehicles, stemming from Micron's pioneering R&D and nanomanufacturing capabilities.

title: High-density MRAM family now commercially available
summary: Everspin Technologies Inc. has commercially launched its EMxxLX family of high-density MRAM devices, touted as the highest performing persistent memory available. These MRAM devices offer superior performance compared to traditional memories like SRAM and NOR flash, suitable for diverse applications including industrial IoT, aerospace, and medical systems. The technology boasts low latency, non-volatility, high endurance, and radiation resistance, with densities ranging from 8 to 64 megabits.

title: AMD Unveils World’s Most Advanced Gaming Graphics Cards, Built on Groundbreaking AMD RDNA 3 Architecture with Chiplet Design
summary: AMD has introduced its new Radeon RX 7900 XTX and Radeon RX 7900 XT graphics cards, based on the RDNA 3 architecture featuring a chiplet design. These cards are poised to deliver exceptional 4K and higher resolution gaming performance with significant improvements in performance per watt, AI throughput, and raytracing compared to the previous generation. AMD also announced updates to its software suite, including FSR 2.2 and the upcoming FSR 3, alongside the expansion of the AMD Advantage framework to desktop PCs.

title: BDA advises onsemi on sale of Niigata foundry to Mercuria, Sangyo Sosei, Fukuoka Capital
summary: BDA Partners advised onsemi on the sale of its Niigata wafer manufacturing facility in Japan to investment vehicles managed by Mercuria Holdings Co Ltd, Sangyo Sosei Advisory Inc, and Fukuoka Capital Partners Co Ltd. This transaction will create Japan's first independent specialty semiconductor foundry. The sale is expected to close upon regulatory approvals and fulfillment of closing conditions.

title: OSS Delivers Ruggedized Compute Solution for Embark's Commercial Autonomous Truck Deployment
summary: One Stop Systems, Inc. (OSS) has secured two program wins with Embark Trucks, Inc., an autonomous trucking technology developer, to provide ruggedized compute solutions for its commercial autonomous truck deployment. These wins include the Centauri rugged PCIe storage expansion system and the 3U SDS-AT rugged accelerated computing server, both designed to withstand harsh environments and support critical autonomous driving operations. This partnership will equip Embark’s test fleet with high-performance computing and transportable storage capabilities, contributing to the advancement of autonomous long-haul trucking.

title: Intel Says Its On Track With “Aggressive” 2030 Foundry Goals, Aiming The 2nd Spot By The End of The Decade
summary: Intel is aggressively pursuing its 2030 foundry goals, aiming to become the world's second-largest chip manufacturer, surpassing Samsung and closing in on TSMC. The company is investing significantly in new fabrication plants globally, developing next-gen process technologies, and building capacity to attract clients and diversify the semiconductor supply chain. Intel plans to achieve this through strategic investments, global expansion, and strong competition against its rivals.

title: What to expect during AMD’s 4th Gen EPYC Genoa Release: Join theCUBE Nov. 10
summary: AMD is set to release its 4th Gen EPYC Genoa CPU on November 10, entering a competitive x86 processor market. The new chips are expected to feature 96 cores and 192 threads based on a 5-nanometer manufacturing process, offering higher density and power efficiency. This launch positions AMD ahead of competitor Intel, which has delayed its Xeon Scalable CPUs.

title: Samsung, LG gear up for launch of 8K gaming monitors
summary: Samsung and LG are preparing to launch 8K gaming monitors, with Samsung unveiling its Odyssey Neo G9 at CES 2023. This move comes as gamers seek larger, clearer screens and high-performance GPUs enable 8K quality. However, high prices and a lack of compatible games remain challenges for mainstream adoption, despite an expected significant growth in the 8K device market.

title: Exclusive: Nvidia offers new advanced chip for China that meets U.S. export controls
summary: Nvidia has developed a new advanced chip, the A800, specifically for the Chinese market, which complies with recent U.S. export control rules designed to restrict cutting-edge technology to China. This move by Nvidia (NVDA.O) is a strategic effort to maintain its presence in the significant Chinese market after U.S. regulations impacted sales of its A100 chip, with the A800 featuring a reduced data transfer rate to meet the export requirements. Analysts suggest this is a "repackaged A100 GPU" demonstrating Nvidia's adaptability to trade restrictions.

title: GlobalFoundries delivers record earnings on sustained demand for lower-end commodity chips
summary: GlobalFoundries Inc. reported record third-quarter financial results, exceeding Wall Street expectations due to sustained demand for lower-end commodity chips, particularly in the automotive and IoT industries. The company achieved record revenue of $2.07 billion and an impressive operating margin of 17.2%, despite concerns in the broader chip market. GlobalFoundries also provided optimistic guidance for the fourth quarter, anticipating strong earnings and revenue.

title: Intel debuts powerful Max Series CPUs and GPUs for high-performance computing and AI
summary: Intel has launched its new Max Series CPUs and GPUs, designed to compete in the high-performance computing (HPC) and artificial intelligence (AI) markets against rivals like AMD and Nvidia. These new chips will power the upcoming Aurora supercomputer at Argonne National Laboratory, aiming to make HPC and AI more accessible for solving complex global challenges. Intel claims its Max Series offers significant performance advantages and better power efficiency compared to competitive silicon.

title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise Extends Supercomputing to the Enterprise with New HPE Cray Portfolio
summary: Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) is expanding its supercomputing portfolio with new HPE Cray EX and HPE Cray XD supercomputers, making exascale-class technology more accessible and affordable for enterprises. These systems are designed to help businesses across various industries, including energy, finance, healthcare, and manufacturing, accelerate R&D, AI, and machine learning initiatives. The new offerings provide significant performance and AI-at-scale capabilities in smaller form factors, supporting advanced modeling, simulation, and data-intensive workloads in traditional data centers.

title: AMD launches data center CPU 'Genoa', taps Google, Microsoft as customers (Nov. 10)
summary: AMD has launched its fourth-generation EPYC processor, codenamed "Genoa," for data centers, securing major customers like Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, and Oracle.
